channel.title: Recommendations on Daniels page
channel.description: Recent content in Recommendations on Daniels page

item.title: Favorites: Summer 2018
item.description: Some of my favorites from summer 2018. There we go. Birds Jaume Sanchez Elias posts amazing geometric animations. Mike Cook thinks about gaming, procedural content generation and computational creativity (he also wrote a book about twitter-bots, which I have not read yet, but looks great). Krieg und Freitag is one of the funniest accounts I came across (German). Severe-weather.eu collects reports of sever-weather/natural-catastrophes around Europe. Kate Compton. Gabriel Peyré provides all kind of info and visualizations about optimal transport.

item.title: Favourites: Spring 2018
item.description: Here are some of my favourites from Spring 2018. www I build a twitter-bot with Cheap Bots, Done Quick. It tweets proverbs, heuristics, fallacies and biases in the wayne of the seminal work of Tversky and Kahneman, Gerd Gigerenzer & co. Ralph Hammer started an amazing series of articles about learning to draw (and by implication see). Starting with an exposition about seeing vs. reading. Now, why he considers “writing software” as a low-dopamine activity is beyond me.

item.title: Media Diet Dump
item.description: John Le Carré - The Pigeon Tunnel. Reading Carrè is always a pleasure. This one is suprisingly self-reflexive. Matthias Adolfosn. Playfull and witty illustration. Can’t get enough of them. Kosko - Neural Networks and Fuzzy Systems: An oldie. Intersting to get a grasp of the historical developement of artifical neural networks and fuzzy logic. Song of the Sea. <3 Patterson. Don’t get fooled by the trailer. The movie is very slow; almost meditative.

item.title: Visualizing Uncertainty
item.description: I am not sure where one should start with this topic. Visualizing uncertainty is hard. in general, it is still an unsolved problem. Back in the days even the mighty Nathan Yau wrote: I still struggle with uncertainty and visualization. I haven’t seen many worthwhile solutions other than the old standbys, boxplots and histograms, which show distributions. But how many people understand spread, skew, etc? It’s a small proportion, which poses an interesting challenge.
